name: Monfragüe National Park Loop Trail
name_original: Casas de Miravete desde Villarreal de San Carlos – circular por el río Tejo – Parque Nacional de Monfragüe
description: This difficult 72.9-mile road cycling route through Monfragüe National Park offers stunning views and a challenging ride.
region_intro: Road cycling in Monfragüe offers routes through a landscape characterized by the Tagus and Tiétar rivers, featuring rolling hills, Mediterranean forests, and dehesas. The region's terrain includes both demanding climbs and winding descents, with many routes on paved surfaces. Cyclists can explore areas with cork and holm oak trees, river gorges, and impressive rock formations. The network of no traffic road cycling routes provides diverse options for exploring this natural environment. Best no traffic road cycling routes in Monfragüe The most popular no traffic road cycling route is Torrejón el Rubio from Monroy — circular by the Embalse de Santa Ana, a 37.2 miles (59.9 km) moderate trail that takes about 2 hours 47 minutes to complete, offering views of the Santa Ana reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cañada Real Soriana Occidental – Serradilla loop from Serradilla, a moderate 31.8 miles (51.1 km) path. This route winds through scenic dehesas and offers a circular journey from Serradilla. Local road cyclists also love the Higuera de Albalat to Campillo de Deleitosa — circular via the Puerto de Campillo, a 22.1 miles (35.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain including the Puerto de Campillo, often completed in about 2 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling in Monfragüe is defined by rolling hills, Mediterranean forests, and river valleys, with routes offering both challenging climbs and scenic descents. The network provides options for different ability levels, primarily featuring moderate routes. The routes in Monfragüe are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Monfragüe's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no traffic road cycling routes are available in Monfragüe?
A: There are currently 3 dedicated no traffic road cycling routes listed in Monfragüe, all rated as moderate difficulty. These routes offer a fantastic way to explore the region's natural beauty without vehicle interference.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see on these routes?
A: The no traffic road cycling routes in Monfragüe traverse diverse landscapes. You'll cycle through areas characterized by dramatic river gorges, rolling hills, and expansive Mediterranean forests and scrubland. Expect to see traditional 'dehesas' with scattered oaks, and enjoy views of the Tagus and Tiétar rivers.

Q: Are there any circular no traffic road cycling routes in Monfragüe?
A: Yes, all the no traffic road cycling routes featured in this guide are circular. For example, you can enjoy the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1284691288/torrejon-el-rubio-from-monroy-circular-by-the-embalse-de-santa-ana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torrejón el Rubio from Monroy — circular by the Embalse de Santa Ana&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a substantial loop through the region.

Q: What is the best time of year for no traffic road biking in Monfragüe?
A: Monfragüe is well-suited for road cycling during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. These seasons also offer vibrant natural scenery and are ideal for wildlife observation, especially birdwatching, without the intense heat of summer.

Q: What are the typical distances and elevation gains for these routes?
A: The no traffic road cycling routes in Monfragüe vary in length and elevation. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371591359/higuera-de-albalat-to-campillo-de-deleitosa-circular-via-the-puerto-de-campillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Higuera de Albalat to Campillo de Deleitosa — circular via the Puerto de Campillo&lt;/a&gt; is about 35.5 km with around 750 meters of elevation gain, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1284691288/torrejon-el-rubio-from-monroy-circular-by-the-embalse-de-santa-ana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torrejón el Rubio from Monroy — circular by the Embalse de Santa Ana&lt;/a&gt; covers nearly 60 km with over 400 meters of ascent.

Q: Can I spot wildlife while road cycling in Monfragüe?
A: Absolutely! Monfragüe is renowned for its rich biodiversity. While cycling, you have an excellent chance to observe majestic birds of prey like griffon vultures, black vultures, and Spanish imperial eagles soaring overhead. Keep an eye out for deer and Iberian pigs in the dehesas as well.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the no traffic road cycling routes?
A: Monfragüe offers numerous strategically located viewpoints. While specific viewpoints may vary by route, the region is home to iconic spots like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323985" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Serrano Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/808762" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from Cerro Gimio&lt;/a&gt;, providing breathtaking panoramic views of the Tagus River and the surrounding Mediterranean forest.

Q: What cultural or historical attractions can I visit near the cycling routes?
A: Beyond its natural beauty, Monfragüe features historical sites. You can visit &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/429392" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monfragüe Castle&lt;/a&gt;, an Arab-built fortress offering majestic views. The small village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4458065" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarreal de San Carlos&lt;/a&gt; within the park also serves as a central point for visitors with interpretation centers.

Q: What do other road cyclists think about the routes in Monfragüe?
A: The road cycling experience in Monfragüe is highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the stunning natural scenery, the quiet roads, and the opportunity for wildlife observation.

Q: Are there options for parking at the trailheads for these routes?
A: While specific parking details for each trailhead are best checked on the individual route pages, Monfragüe National Park and its surrounding villages, such as Villarreal de San Carlos and Serradilla, generally offer visitor parking facilities suitable for accessing the cycling routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133005" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada Real Soriana Occidental – Serradilla loop from Serradilla&lt;/a&gt; starts directly from a village with amenities.

Q: Are there any natural monuments to see along the way?
A: Yes, Monfragüe is home to impressive natural monuments. A prominent one is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5679306" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Gypsy's Leap&lt;/a&gt; (Salto del Gitano), a dramatic gorge where the Tagus River cuts through quartzite cliffs, offering breathtaking views and prime birdwatching opportunities.
tips: Puente del Cardenal was built around 1450 to allow the creation of a trade route across the Tagus River. Located at the foot of the Monfragüe Castle, the historic bridge is one of the highlights of the Monfragüe Biosphere Reserve.
Ideal and indispensable place to see the birds of the area.

Jaraicejo is a small town of medieval origin in the Monfragüe region between the Sierra de Piatones, the Monfragüe National Park and the Almonte River. The Parish Church of the Assumption towers over the town and can be seen from afar.
Both the views of the bridge over the river and the panoramic view you have of the Tagus when you cross it, from the siding, justify a stop on your route.
Small settlement to pause. There is an information film about the region and information about the native birds.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route, covering approximately 117 kilometers, typically takes around 6 hours and 9 minutes to complete. This duration is for cycling time only and does not include stops for rest or sightseeing.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult. You should be prepared for significant elevation changes, with around 1,342 meters of ascent and descent. The terrain in Monfragüe National Park features rugged landscapes, winding roads, and sustained climbs, requiring good physical fitness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows paved roads through the rugged and varied landscape of Monfragüe National Park. Expect a mix of ascents, descents, and winding sections. While the main roads are paved, some inclines can be demanding. You'll cycle through Mediterranean forests, extensive dehesas (oak woodlands), and alongside dramatic river gorges carved by the Tagus (Tejo) River.

faqs.question: What natural features and wildlife might I see while cycling?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in the heart of Monfragüe National Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. Expect stunning Mediterranean landscapes, ancient dehesas, and picturesque riverside stretches along the Tejo River. The park is renowned for its birdlife, so keep an eye out for large birds of prey such as black storks, griffon vultures, Egyptian vultures, golden eagles, and peregrine falcons soaring above the cliffs.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to cycle this route are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be chilly and wet.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers several significant points of interest. You'll cross the historic Puente del Cardenal on the Tagus River and the Almaraz Bridge over the Tagus River. You'll also pass by the Peña Falcón (Gypsy’s Leap), which offers breathtaking panoramas of the Tagus River. The route also takes you through Villarreal de San Carlos, a key access point for the park, and near the Parish Church of the Assumption of Jaraicejo.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Villarreal de San Carlos serves as a central hub and offers parking facilities. It's a convenient starting point for exploring Monfragüe National Park and accessing this circular route.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several official trails. You will find yourself on parts of the Camino Natural del Tajo, Camino de Monfragüe - Etapa 3, and Camino Mozárabe por Trujillo. Smaller sections also coincide with the Ruta del Castillo de Monfragüe and Ruta de Mirador de la Tajadilla.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in Monfragüe National Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on the public roads within Monfragüe National Park. However, as a protected area, it's essential to respect all park regulations, stay on marked routes, and avoid disturbing wildlife or natural habitats.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, significant distance (117 km), and substantial elevation gain (1,342 m), this specific route is not recommended for beginners or families with young children. It requires a good level of fitness and experience in road cycling. Shorter, less challenging routes might be available elsewhere in the park for those seeking an easier experience.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in Monfragüe National Park, specific regulations apply, especially concerning protected wildlife areas. On a road cycling route like this, dogs should always be kept on a leash and under strict control. It's advisable to check the latest park regulations regarding pets before your visit, particularly concerning access to certain viewpoints or natural monuments.

Parish Church of the Assumption of Jaraicejo
